name: Sloan Peak
note: The supplied parentArea "Glacier Peak Wilderness" appears to be incorrect. Multiple sources (PeakVisor's Henry M. Jackson Wilderness page, USFS) identify Sloan Peak as the highest peak within the Henry M. Jackson Wilderness, not the Glacier Peak Wilderness — the two wildernesses are adjacent (Glacier Peak Wilderness borders Henry M. Jackson Wilderness to the north/east), and Sloan Peak sits on the Henry M. Jackson side of that boundary. Name, coordinates (48.04145, -121.340261 ≈ Wikipedia's 48°02'29"N 121°20'25"W), and elevation (7,835 ft) all check out and unambiguously identify the correct, real peak.

name: Del Campo Peak
note: Confirmed genuine: coordinates (47.9907, -121.470477) match Wikipedia's 47°59'27"N 121°28'14"W for Del Campo Peak in Snohomish County, and "Monte Cristo Group" as parent area is geographically correct (the Mountaineers/other sources place it in the "Monte Cristo / Barlow Pass Area," directly above Gothic Basin near the historic Monte Cristo mining district). No mismatch found. Minor data point: elevation is most commonly sourced as 6,610 ft (Wikipedia, WTA, Peakbagger-derived) vs. the given 6,615 ft — a 5 ft discrepancy, noted in the route's corrections field.

name: Cathedral Rock
note: Peak identity is solid: Wikipedia's coordinates for Cathedral Rock, WA (47°33'19"N 121°08'08"W ≈ 47.5553,-121.1356) and its 6,724 ft elevation match the given data almost exactly, and it is a well-documented, unambiguous summit (not to be confused with Cathedral Peak in the Pasayten, or Cathedral Rocks in Yosemite/Colorado). However, the given parentArea "Snoqualmie Pass" is geographically off: Cathedral Rock sits above the Cle Elum River valley near Salmon la Sac/Roslyn, accessed via FR-4330 and the Cathedral Pass Trail out of the Cle Elum Ranger District (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Alpine Lakes Wilderness, Kittitas County) — roughly 15-20 road miles from the actual Snoqualmie Pass (I-90 summit). Mountain Project itself files it under "Central-East Cascades, Wenatchee & Leavenworth," not the Snoqualmie Pass Area. Both are part of the broader Central Cascades/Alpine Lakes region, so this may be an intentional coarse grouping, but the parent label does not reflect the real trailhead/approach geography and may be worth revisiting.

name: Mount Pulitzer (Wellesley Peak)
note: The given name "Mount Pulitzer (Wellesley Peak)" appears to conflate two DIFFERENT Bailey Range summits. Wellesley Peak is a separate, higher peak (~6,758-6,759 ft, ~301 m prominence) located about 14 miles east at approximately 47.791066, -123.291099 (per PeakVisor/SummitPost Olympic Range prominence lists) — it is not an alternate name for Mount Pulitzer. The given coordinates (47.835525, -123.574815) and elevation (6283 ft) match Mount Pulitzer precisely against Wikipedia's sourced data (47.83559N, 123.574217W, 6,283 ft), confirming this DB row genuinely is Mount Pulitzer. Mount Pulitzer's actual documented alternate name is "Snagtooth," not Wellesley Peak. Recommend dropping "(Wellesley Peak)" from the display name or, if Wellesley Peak is meant to exist as its own catalog entry, adding it separately rather than as an alias here. The parent area "Central Olympic Mountains" is geographically plausible in a loose sense (Mount Pulitzer sits in the Bailey Range, a subrange near the center of Olympic National Park), though the precise subrange name used by all sources is "Bailey Range."

name: Agnes Mountain
note: Peak identity checks out at the coordinate level: the given lat/lng (48.3176, -120.9685) match Wikipedia's cited position for Agnes Mountain (48°19'03"N 120°58'03"W ≈ 48.3175, -120.9675), and the elevation (8,131 ft given) is consistent with the range of values reputable sources report (8,115 ft per WTA/Wikipedia summary, 8,119 ft NAVD88 per Wikipedia infobox, 8,133 ft per ListsOfJohn) — so the peak record itself is sound. However, the assigned parent area "Darrington and Mountain Loop Hwy" appears to be a hierarchy mismatch. Every source describing this peak places it in the Agnes Creek drainage accessed from Stehekin (Lake Chelan), reached only by the Lady of the Lake ferry across Lake Chelan and then a shuttle/hike up the roadless Stehekin Valley to High Bridge — a Chelan County location on the east side of the Cascade crest, within/adjacent to the Glacier Peak Wilderness (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest) and the Lake Chelan National Recreation Area (North Cascades National Park Complex). Darrington and the Mountain Loop Highway are on the west side of the range in Snohomish County, roughly 50+ miles away with no shared access corridor. This peak likely belongs under a Stehekin/Lake Chelan/Glacier Peak Wilderness parent area rather than Darrington/Mountain Loop Hwy.

name: East Face
note: Confirmed as a real, well-documented place: Mountain Project's own GPS for this crag (47.3651, -121.31359) is an exact match to the given coordinates, and several individual route pages (With Love Lie Ancients Nestled Within, Buckets Full of Moonlight Lost, Glacier View Temple) independently list the identical GPS. However, note this is NOT a summit/alpine peak — "East Face" is a climbing sub-sector (one of three: West Wall, Lizard Ledge, East Face) of Keechelus Ridge, a single-pitch/short-multipitch sport-and-trad crag at roughly 4,400-4,700 ft on the forested ridge between Keechelus and Kachess Lakes, just east of Snoqualmie Pass. The parent "Keechelus Ridge" is geographically correct. No elevation figure was given for this node (correctly left null) since it's a crag sector rather than a discrete summit; Mountain Project's crag-base elevation is ~4,418 ft (1,347 m). Peakbagger/SummitPost have essentially no coverage of this location since it isn't a prominence-listed peak — Mountain Project, theCrag/openbeta cross-references, and The Mountaineers are the load-bearing sources here instead.

name: South Early Winters Spire
note: Minor coordinate note only — name, elevation, and parent hierarchy all check out cleanly (Liberty Bell Group / Washington Pass / North Cascades; 7,807 ft confirmed by Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger, and Wikipedia). However, the given peak coordinates (48.512275, -120.655395) sit ~0.4-0.5 mi south of Peakbagger's summit coordinate (48.5167653, -120.6573362), and instead closely match Mountain Project's route-anchor GPS pin (48.51216, -120.65513) used on all of this peak's individual route pages. This suggests the stored lat/lng may be an approach/route-cluster point rather than the true summit — worth a spot-check against Peakbagger if summit-precision coordinates matter downstream.

name: Dikes, The
note: "The Dikes" is not a summit — it's a cluster of short basalt dike-wall sport crags (Megadike North/South, Minidike, The Castle) on the flank of Chase Mountain in the Blue Mountains, Umatilla National Forest. The given coordinates (46.12344,-117.81893) and name match Mountain Project's "The Dikes" area (id 107487344) and Kevin Pogue's Whitman College guidebook exactly, so the record is correctly identified. Two notes: (1) the county is Columbia County, WA, not Walla Walla County — "Walla Walla Ranger District" is just the USFS administrative unit name; the East Fork Touchet drainage here is south of Dayton, the Columbia County seat. (2) elevationFt is null in the given data; Mountain Project shows ~3,743 ft for the general area pin and ~3,892 ft for the Megadike North subarea specifically, but since this is a multi-wall crag rather than a single summit, no single "peak elevation" cleanly applies — treat any elevation figure here as an approximate crag-location elevation, not a summit height. No PeakBagger/SummitPost entries exist for "The Dikes" because it isn't a baggable summit, which is expected for a crag and not a data problem.

name: Pernod Spire
note: The stored coordinates (48.55, -120.595) sit roughly 3 miles northeast of Mountain Project's listed GPS for Pernod Spire's own area page (48.5123, -120.654), which places it just south of Washington Pass — consistent with the AAC's description of the Wine Spires as "at the fringe of the glacier north of Silver Star's North Peak" and with the approach description (Hwy 20 pullout ~0.7 mi past Cutthroat Lake Rd, ~26 mi from Winthrop). The name, elevation range, and parent area (Washington Pass) are otherwise consistent with a real, identifiable peak; recommend re-verifying the stored lat/lng against Mountain Project/topo data.

name: Red Mountain
note: Peak identity confirmed: Wikipedia's coordinates for Red Mountain (King County, WA), 47°27'22"N 121°23'21"W, convert to 47.4561,-121.3892 — an essentially exact match to the given lat/lng (47.456135,-121.389105), and its elevation (5,890 ft) and parent area (Snoqualmie Pass / Alpine Lakes Wilderness) are correct and well-documented. HOWEVER: one listed route, "Ragged Ridge" (wa_ragged_ridge), does not belong to this peak. Ragged Ridge is a well-documented, geographically distinct North Cascades ridge/traverse (Katsuk, Kimtah, Cosho Peaks) near Easy Pass/Thunder Creek in North Cascades National Park, roughly 50+ miles north of Snoqualmie Pass — no source ties it to this Red Mountain, and Mountain Project's "Red Mountain" area page (the authoritative local route catalog) lists only three climbs: East Ridge, South Face, and Southwest Ridge (Winter). This route entry appears to be a data-entry/attribution error and should be reviewed for removal or reassignment to the correct North Cascades peak. Separately, minor note: Mountain Project's "East Ridge" route page (filed under the Red Mountain area, reached via the same Red Pass approach) is itself titled "East Ridge, Lundin Peak" — Lundin Peak is a distinct, slightly lower neighboring summit "halfway between Snoqualmie Mountain and Red Mountain." Sources are not fully consistent on whether this specific technical line tops out on Red Mountain proper or on Lundin Peak; I've filled the route as documented under the Red Mountain area/approach but flagged this ambiguity in that route's corrections field.

name: Obelisk, The
note: Verified via an exact GPS match (47.36859, -121.45406) to Mountain Project's "The Obelisk" sub-area page, itself nested under an area literally named "The Citidel" (matching this DB's misspelled parentArea) in the Snoqualmie Pass Area of the Central-West Cascades. Flag for future researchers: Washington has two unrelated features both called "Citadel" — (1) this small, non-wilderness sport-climbing formation (~4,585 ft) on Silver Peak's shoulder near Snoqualmie Pass, reached by FS road NF-9070, and (2) a separate, much better-known 7,120-ft alpine summit named "The Citadel" near Cathedral Rock deep in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness (accessed via Tucquala Meadows/Circle Lake), which has its own SummitPost page and is NOT this peak. The coordinate match confirms the given data correctly refers to formation (1), the Snoqualmie Pass crag — no correction needed, just noting the ambiguity so it isn't conflated later.

name: Mossy Loaf
note: Mossy Loaf is correctly filed under "Monte Cristo" (matches Mountain Project's own area hierarchy), and the given coordinates (48.03015, -121.44552) match Mountain Project's Mossy Loaf listing exactly, so placement is verified. However, this is NOT a distinct mountain peak/summit — it is a small (35-45 ft) two-tiered roadside rock wall sitting essentially at Barlow Pass elevation (~2,300-2,360 ft), about 0.4 km (a quarter mile) before the Barlow Pass trailhead parking area on the paved Mountain Loop Highway. It does NOT require the well-known 4-mile hike down the gated Monte Cristo road to the historic ghost-town site — that hike leads to an entirely different, more remote location roughly 4 miles further southeast (Monte Cristo townsite proper, ~47.986, -121.394, elev. ~2,762 ft per Wikipedia). "Summit"-style fields (highPointFt, etc.) are not meaningfully applicable to this crag.

name: Mount Cruiser
note: Peak identity checks out: Wikipedia lists Mount Cruiser at 47°33'52"N 123°18'56"W (≈47.5644, -123.3156) and 6,104 ft — matches the given coordinates/elevation exactly, and it is unambiguously the Sawtooth Ridge summit in the southern Olympic Mountains (Mason County), Mount Skokomish Wilderness, Olympic National Forest, near Flapjack/Mildred/Murdock Lakes. HOWEVER the given parentArea "North-Central Olympic Mountains (Deception-Gray Wolf)" is geographically wrong for this peak. Deception Basin / the Gray Wolf River drainage is a distinct area roughly 25-30 miles to the north-northeast, in the Buckhorn Wilderness / northeastern Olympics near Sequim/Dosewallips — not the same region as the Sawtooth Range / Mount Skokomish Wilderness, which every source (Wikipedia, SummitPost, USFS) places in the southern Olympics near Lake Cushman/Hood Canal, accessed via the Staircase entrance of Olympic National Park. Recommend reparenting Mount Cruiser to a "Southern Olympic Mountains / Mount Skokomish Wilderness (Sawtooth Range)" bucket rather than the Deception-Gray Wolf group.

name: Amphitheater Mountain
note: Correction: the given elevationFt (8374) does not match reliable sourcing — Wikipedia, Mountain Project, Mazamas, and Peakbagger all converge on 8,358 ft (2,548 m) for Amphitheater Mountain. Coordinates given (48.97566, -120.192342) are consistent with Wikipedia's summit coordinate (48.97556, -120.19222) and Mountain Project's area coordinate (48.97698, -120.19208), so no correction needed there. Parent area "Pasayten Wilderness" is correct (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District). Note: summitpost.org returned HTTP 403 on every fetch attempt during this research and could not be used directly; peakbagger.com's page also 403'd on direct fetch, so its data (elevation, prominence, NAD27 coordinates) was corroborated instead via search-result synthesis citing that page, cross-checked against Wikipedia and Mountain Project, which agree.
